title: Critical Reasoning: Weaken
prerequisites: spot the assumption connecting evidence to conclusion
learning_objectives: Find the conclusion, then choose the fact that makes it less likely to be true.
Attack the assumption, or supply an alternative cause for the evidence.
Ignore choices that are irrelevant or that merely fail to help the argument.

pedagogical_model.sequence: I do -> we do -> you do

opening.builds_on: Finding an argument's assumption and how a strengthener supports it.

items.prompt: What is the most common way to weaken a causal claim?
items.answer: Provide an alternative cause for the observed effect.

items.prompt: Does a choice that is merely irrelevant weaken an argument?
items.answer: No - it must make the conclusion less likely.

items.prompt: Weaken or not: the two groups compared differed in another important way.

retrieval_starter.purpose: Recalls that weakening usually means attacking the assumption or offering a rival cause.
retrieval_starter.if_students_struggle: Remind them the correct answer need not disprove the conclusion - just dent it.

questions.prompt: Name two ways to weaken a causal argument.
questions.answer: Break the assumption; give an alternative cause.

questions.prompt: Why doesn't 'the plan is expensive' weaken 'the plan will cut congestion'?
questions.answer: Cost doesn't bear on whether congestion falls.

opening.prior_knowledge_bridge: Strengthening reinforced the assumption; weakening does the opposite - it pries the assumption loose or hands you a different explanation for the evidence. Find the gap, then widen it.
opening.learning_intention: By the end you can pick the choice that most reduces the likelihood the conclusion is true, usually by attacking the assumption or providing an alternative cause.
success_criteria: I can state the conclusion the choice must undercut.
I can tell a real weakener from an irrelevant choice.
I can spot 'here's another cause' as a weakening move.

opening_script.move: Do Now on screen; learners answer from memory, no notes.
Cold-call answers; name the argument's conclusion vs premises.
Bridge to today's question type; state what the stem is really asking.
Move to I do: model the reasoning on one worked item.

i_do.stem: A bakery introduced a rewards card and its sales rose the next month, so the owner concluded the rewards card increased sales. Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the conclusion?

options.A: The bakery's only nearby competitor closed for renovations that same month.
options.B: The rewards card was printed on recycled paper.
options.C: Some customers forgot to bring their rewards card.
options.D: The bakery had offered a rewards card once before.
options.E: The bakery sells both bread and pastries.

i_do.explanation: A competitor closing for the month offers an alternative cause for the sales bump, undermining the claim that the rewards card was responsible.
think_aloud_steps: Conclusion: the rewards card caused higher sales (causal).
To weaken a cause, find another explanation for the effect.
A competitor closing that month explains the rise without the card.
The other choices are irrelevant to what caused the increase.
i_do.key_takeaway: For causal conclusions, the strongest weakener usually supplies an alternative cause for the evidence.

we_do.stem: A manager concluded that a new training program improved her team's sales, since sales rose after the training. Which of the following, if true, most weakens the conclusion?

options.A: The company launched a large price promotion the same week as the training.
options.B: The training program lasted two full days.
options.C: Some team members had attended similar training before.
options.D: The trainer was hired from outside the company.
options.E: Sales are tracked by an automated system.

we_do.explanation: A simultaneous price promotion is an alternative cause for the sales rise, so the training may not deserve the credit.
scaffold_hints: The claim is causal: training -> higher sales.
Look for something else that could have raised sales at the same time.
Which choice offers a competing explanation?

immediate_feedback.if_correct: Yes - a concurrent promotion undercuts the causal claim.
immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: Details about the trainer or tracking don't change what caused the rise.

we_do.stem: A health official argued that a public-awareness campaign reduced smoking, because smoking rates fell after the campaign. Which of the following, if true, most weakens the argument?

options.A: A steep national tax on cigarettes took effect at the same time as the campaign.
options.B: The campaign used billboards and radio ads.
options.C: Some smokers said they never saw the campaign.
options.D: The campaign was funded by a health charity.
options.E: Smoking rates are measured through annual surveys.

we_do.explanation: A simultaneous cigarette tax is a well-known driver of reduced smoking, giving an alternative cause for the decline and weakening the campaign's claimed effect.
scaffold_hints: What else, happening at the same time, is known to cut smoking?
That competing cause weakens the campaign's claim.
Ad formats and funding don't address causation.

immediate_feedback.if_correct: Right - the tax is a strong alternative cause.
immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: 'Some never saw it' is minor; a nationwide tax is a much bigger competing cause.

you_do.stem: A store owner concluded that moving the checkout counter increased impulse purchases, since such purchases rose after the move. Which of the following, if true, most weakens the conclusion?

options.A: The store began stocking popular candy and magazines near the checkout at the same time.
options.B: The new counter is made of a more durable material.
options.C: The move took place over a single weekend.
options.E: Impulse purchases are recorded at the register.

you_do.explanation: Adding tempting items by the checkout is an alternative cause for the rise in impulse buys, independent of the counter's location.

you_do.stem: A blogger claims that switching to a standing desk increased her productivity, noting she got more done after the switch. Which of the following, if true, most weakens the claim?

options.A: She also started using a new task-planning app the week she got the standing desk.
options.B: The standing desk was more expensive than her old desk.
options.C: She sometimes lowers the desk to sit.
options.D: Standing desks are increasingly common in offices.
options.E: She measures productivity by tasks completed.

you_do.explanation: Adopting a new planning app at the same time provides an alternative cause for the productivity gain, undermining the desk's claimed effect.

you_do.stem: A city concluded that a new after-school program reduced youth crime, because such crime fell after the program opened. Which of the following, if true, most weakens the conclusion?

options.A: During the same period, the city hired dozens of additional police officers to patrol youth areas.
options.B: The program offers sports and tutoring.
options.C: The program is open four days a week.
options.D: Attendance at the program varies by season.
options.E: Youth crime is reported by local schools.

you_do.explanation: A simultaneous surge in policing is an alternative cause for the drop in youth crime, weakening the claim that the program was responsible.

mastery_check.definition: A topic is mastered at >=85% correct on new/held-out-style questions across >=2 spaced sessions with delay (PRD Section 9.2). Flashcard recall alone is not mastery; application under mixed, delayed conditions is the bar.
conditions: >=85% correct on new/held-out-style items (not flashcard recall) per session
sustained across >=2 spaced sessions with a delay between them
per-topic memory surfaced only after >=20 graded reviews (PRD give-up rule)
per-topic performance surfaced only after >=8 application attempts (PRD give-up rule)
mastery_check.on_fail: Not yet: route every miss to the required error log (tag why: content gap / reasoning flaw / timing / careless / trap), schedule spaced re-practice via topic-aware scheduling, then re-enter the we-do -> you-do loop before re-testing.
